module: a1-009
level: A1
sequence: 9
slug: this-is-i-am
version: '2.0'
title: This Is / I Am
subtitle: Zero Copula and Personal Pronouns
duration: 45
transliteration: false
tags: [grammar, pronouns, identity]
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
content_outline:
- section: 'Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)'
  words: 200
- section: 'Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)'
  words: 275
- section: 'Граматика: Секрет нульової зв''язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)'
  words: 350
- section: Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)
  words: 200
- section: 'Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)'
  words: 175
---

You've spent eight modules learning the Ukrainian alphabet, sounds, gender, and your first polite phrases. Now it's time for something exciting — you're going to learn how to say who you are, ask who someone else is, and do it all without the word "is." Welcome to the zero copula — your Ukrainian superpower.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to introduce yourself, identify people and objects, and use all eight Ukrainian personal pronouns. Let's go!

## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є студент»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є студент.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я студент.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є студент»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
FIN

- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є студент»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є студент.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я студент.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є студент»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
END

  words: 175
---

You've spent eight modules learning the Ukrainian alphabet, sounds, gender, and your first polite phrases. Now it's time for something exciting — you're going to learn how to say who you are, ask who someone else is, and do it all without the word "is." Welcome to the zero copula — your Ukrainian superpower.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to introduce yourself, identify people and objects, and use all eight Ukrainian personal pronouns. Let's go!

## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є студент»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є студент.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я студент.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є студент»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
FINISH

  words: 200

  words: 175
---

You've spent eight modules learning the Ukrainian alphabet, sounds, gender, and your first polite phrases. Now it's time for something exciting — you're going to learn how to say who you are, ask who someone else is, and do it all without the word "is." Welcome to the zero copula — your Ukrainian superpower.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to introduce yourself, identify people and objects, and use all eight Ukrainian personal pronouns. Let's go!

## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є студент»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є студент.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я студент.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є студент»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
FIN

cat <<'FIN' > curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/this-is-i-am.md
---
module: a1-009
level: A1
sequence: 9
slug: this-is-i-am
version: '2.0'
title: This Is / I Am
subtitle: Zero Copula and Personal Pronouns
duration: 45
transliteration: false
tags: [grammar, pronouns, identity]
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Learner can use personal pronouns (я, ти, він, вона, etc.)
- Learner can form identity statements without 'to be' (zero copula)
grammar:
- Personal pronouns
- Zero copula construction
content_outline:
- section: 'Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)'
  words: 200
- section: 'Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)'
  words: 275
- section: 'Граматика: Секрет нульової зв''язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)'
  words: 350
- section: Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)
  words: 200
- section: 'Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)'
  words: 175
---

You've spent eight modules learning the Ukrainian alphabet, sounds, gender, and your first polite phrases. Now it's time for something exciting — you're going to learn how to say who you are, ask who someone else is, and do it all without the word "is." Welcome to the zero copula — your Ukrainian superpower.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to introduce yourself, identify people and objects, and use all eight Ukrainian personal pronouns. Let's go!

## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є student»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є student.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я student.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є student»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
FIN

printf -- "---

  words: 200

  words: 275

  words: 350
- section: Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)
  words: 200
- section: 'Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)'
  words: 175
---

You've spent eight modules learning the Ukrainian alphabet, sounds, gender, and your first polite phrases. Now it's time for something exciting — you're going to learn how to say who you are, ask who someone else is, and do it all without the word "is." Welcome to the zero copula — your Ukrainian superpower.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to introduce yourself, identify people and objects, and use all eight Ukrainian personal pronouns. Let's go!

## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти студент?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є student»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є student.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я student.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Error Correction and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*!

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Production: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Your Self-Introduction Pattern

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є student»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)

args.old_string:
## Вступ: Хто це? Що це? (Introduction: Who is this? What is this?)

Imagine you're walking through a Ukrainian classroom on your first day. Someone points to a person at the door and asks **Хто це?** (Who is this?). You look at the desk and wonder **Що це?** (What is this?). These two simple questions open the door to real conversation.

The word **це** (this is) is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It works like a spotlight — point it at something and name it:

- **Це Ірина.** — This is Iryna.
- **Це книга.** — This is a book.
- **Це кіт.** — This is a cat.
- **Це хліб.** — This is bread.

The pattern is beautifully simple: **це** + a noun = a complete sentence. No verb needed!

Ukrainian uses two question words to ask about identity. Use **хто** (who) for people and animals, and **що** (what) for objects:

> **(In a classroom / У класі)**
>
> — Хто це?
>
> — Це Тарас.
>
> — А що це?
>
> — Це підручник.

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1, p. 23 -->

[!tip] **Quick Win**
You already know **це** from earlier modules — remember **«Це кіт»** and **«Це молоко»**? You've been using this pattern all along. Now you understand why it works!

When you meet someone new, you'll want to share your name. Ukrainian has a special formula for this: **Мене звати...** (My name is...). This phrase is used everywhere — at school, at work, when meeting new people:

- **Мене звати Оксана.** — My name is Oksana.
- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.

Try it right now with your own name: **Мене звати ______.**

## Презентація: Особові займенники (Presentation: Personal Pronouns)

Now that you can point at things with **це**, let's learn the words that replace names — personal pronouns. English has "I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they." Ukrainian has the same set of eight:

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |
| **ти** | you (informal) | one friend or child |
| **він** | he | replaces masculine nouns |
| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |
| **ми** | we | the speaker + others |
| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 4, p. 90 -->

Ukrainian Grade 4 textbooks define pronouns as words that "point to a person or thing but don't name them." That's exactly what you're learning — these eight words let you talk about anyone without repeating their name.

### The «Ви» Safety Net — Formal vs Informal

Here's something your English doesn't prepare you for. Ukrainian has TWO words for "you" — **ти** (informal) and **ви** (formal or plural). This is not optional; it's a core part of Ukrainian social life.

**Your rule as a beginner: always start with «ви».** Use **ви** with strangers, older people, teachers, doctors, and officials. Use **ти** only with close friends, children, and family members.

> **(At a doctor's office / У лікарні)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Марія. Я лікарка. А ви хто?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!

Notice the doctor uses **ви**, not **ти**. This is the safe default for every new interaction.

[!culture] **Switching to "ти" — The Bruderschaft Moment**
Switching from **ви** to **ти** is a real social milestone. Someone might ask **«Перейдемо на ти?»** (Shall we switch to "ти"?) or simply suggest using the informal "ти" — that's an invitation to a closer relationship. Until you hear those words, stick with **ви**. You can never go wrong with **ви**, but using **ти** with the wrong person can feel disrespectful.

[!cultural-note] **The Power of the Name**
In Ukrainian culture, names carry weight. When you say **«Мене звати...»**, you aren't just giving a label; you're opening a connection. Ukrainians often use just the first name among friends, but in formal settings, you might hear a first name and a patronymic (a name based on the father's name). As a beginner, your first name is perfect!

[!warning] **Learner Error: Register Mix-Up**
English speakers often use **ти** with everyone because English only has "you." If you're speaking with your teacher, a shop assistant, or an older neighbour — always use **ви**. When in doubt, **ви** is your safety net.

[!example] **Pronouns in Action**
Let's see how pronouns replace names in a simple story:
- **Це Тарас.** (This is Taras.) → **Він тут.** (He is here.)
- **Це книга.** (This is a book.) → **Вона там.** (It is there.)
- **Це вікно.** (This is a window.) → **Воно ось.** (It is here.)
- **Тарас і я.** (Taras and I.) → **Ми друзі.** (We are friends.)
- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

## Граматика: Секрет нульової зв'язки (Grammar: The Zero Copula Secret)

Here's the big surprise of this module. In English, you need the verb "to be" in every identity sentence:

- I **am** a student.
- She **is** a teacher.
- He **is** Ukrainian.

In Ukrainian, that little verb simply disappears. Ukrainian drops "to be" in the present tense. Linguists call this the "zero copula" — a fancy name for "no linking verb." Look:

| English | Ukrainian | What's missing? |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| I **am** a student. | **Я Ø студент.** | am |
| She **is** a teacher. | **Вона Ø вчителька.** | is |
| He **is** Ukrainian. | **Він Ø українець.** | is |
| We **are** friends. | **Ми Ø друзі.** | are |

The **Ø** symbol shows where English would put "am/is/are" — but Ukrainian leaves it empty. Subject goes straight to the predicate. That's it!

Here are more examples:

- **Я студент.** — I am a student. (male speaker)
- **Я студентка.** — I am a student. (female speaker)
- **Він вчитель.** — He is a teacher.
- **Вона українка.** — She is Ukrainian.
- **Ти student?** — Are you a student?
- **Ви вчителька?** — Are you (formal) a teacher?
- **Вони друзі.** — They are friends.
- **Ми тут.** — We are here.

[!tip] **The Pattern**
**Pronoun + Noun = Complete sentence.**
No "am," no "is," no "are." Just two words and you're done.

> **(At a conference / На конференції)**
>
> — Хто ви?
>
> — Я Оксана. Я вчителька. А ви?
>
> — Я Тарас. Я студент.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

### The Phantom «є» — A Common Trap

Does Ukrainian have a word for "is"? Yes — **є**. But in modern spoken Ukrainian, **є** is almost never used in identity sentences. Saying *«Я є student»* sounds archaic, like saying "I doth be a student" in English.

[!warning] **Learner Error: The Phantom "Is"**
Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!

- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you've describing!

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>

>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

| Ukrainian | English | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **я** | I | the speaker |

| **вона** | she | replaces feminine nouns |
| **воно** | it | replaces neuter nouns only |

| **ви** | you (formal or plural) | strangers, elders, groups |
| **вони** | they | any group of people or things |

>
> — Дуже приємно!

- **Оксана і Тарас.** (Oksana and Taras.) → **Вони там.** (They are there.)

Your English brain will want to insert **є** into every sentence. Resist the urge!
- ❌ *Я є студент.* — sounds strange and unnatural
- ✅ **Я студент.** — natural and correct
- ❌ *Вона є вчителька.* — awkward
- ✅ **Вона вчителька.** — perfect

In identity statements (saying who someone is or what something is), drop the verb. Just pronoun + noun.

### Nationality and Role Pairs

Ukrainian has different forms for masculine and feminine roles and nationalities. You already know about grammatical gender from Module 7. Now you'll use it with pronouns:

- **Він українець.** / **Вона українка.** — He / She is Ukrainian.
- **Він студент.** / **Вона студентка.** — He / She is a student.
- **Він вчитель.** / **Вона вчителька.** — He / She is a teacher.

A masculine speaker says **Я українець**, a feminine speaker says **Я українка**. Always match the form to the person you're describing!

## Робота над помилками та практика (Common Errors and Practice)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call everything that isn't a person "it" — the table, the book, the car. Ukrainian doesn't work this way! Remember from Module 7: every noun has a grammatical gender. When you replace a noun with a pronoun, you must match the gender:

- **Стіл** (table) is masculine → **він**
- **Книга** (book) is feminine → **вона**
- **Молоко** (milk) is neuter → **воно**

So when someone asks **Що це?** and you point to a table, say **Це стіл. Він тут.** — never *Воно тут*! This is one of the biggest mental shifts for English speakers.

[!note] **When to Use «воно»**
Use **воно** only for neuter nouns: **молоко**, **місто** (city), **вікно** (window), **море** (sea). Don't use **воно** for people — that's rude!

### Drill: Replacing Nouns with Pronouns

Practice replacing each noun with the correct pronoun. Remember the gender!

- **Це Іван.** → **Він студент.**
- **Це Оксана.** → **Вона вчителька.**
- **Це книга.** → **Вона тут.**
- **Це дім.** → **Він там.**
- **Це молоко.** → **Воно тут.**
- **Це сіль.** → **Вона там.**

### Transformation Practice

Now combine **це** identification with pronoun sentences:

- **Це Тарас. Він українець.**
- **Це Марія. Вона студентка.**
- **Це хліб. Він там.**
- **Ось книга. Вона тут.**

The word **ось** (here, over here) works like **це** but draws extra attention — like pointing and saying "right here!" or "look!"

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
>
> — Що це?
>
> — Це книга. Вона тут.
>
> — А що там?
>
> — Там стіл. Він там.
>
> — А хто це?
>
> — Це Марія. Вона студентка.

## Продакшн: Хто я і Хто ви? (Your Intro: Who am I and Who are you?)

You now have everything you need for your first real self-introduction in Ukrainian. Let's put it all together!

### Patterns for Intro

- **Мене звати ______.** (My name is...)
- **Я студент / студентка.** (I am a student.)
- **Я українець / українка.** (I am Ukrainian. — or your nationality)

> **(At a university / В університеті)**
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас. Я студент. Я українець. А ви?
>
> — Добрий день! Мене звати Сара. Я студентка.
>
> — Дуже приємно!
>
> — Дуже приємно!

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Привіт! Я Марія. А ти хто?
>
> — Привіт! Я Іван. Я студент.
>
> — Ти українець?
>
> — Так, я українець. А ти?
>
> — Я українка!

Notice the difference? The first dialogue uses **ви** — a formal meeting at university. The second uses **ти** — chatting with a peer at a café. You're already applying the **Ви Safety Net**!

### Identifying Others

You can also introduce other people:

- **Ось Тарас. Він студент.**
- **Ось Оксана. Вона вчителька.**
- **Вони тут. Ми друзі.**

The simple structure **Subject + Noun** (no verb!) is the foundation of your A1 communication. You'll use it hundreds of times from here on.

## Summary

You've just unlocked one of the most important structures in Ukrainian. Here's what you can now do:

- **Identify** people and things: **Це Ірина. Це книга.**
- **Ask** who or what: **Хто це? Що це?**
- **Introduce yourself**: **Мене звати... Я студент.**
- **Use all 8 pronouns**: **я, ти, він, вона, воно, ми, ви, вони**
- **Form sentences without "to be"**: **Я українець. Вона вчителька.**
- **Choose the right register**: **ви** for strangers, **ти** for friends

The zero copula is your new superpower. English speakers need three words ("I am a student"), but you need just two: **Я студент.** Clean, simple, powerful. Great work — you're well on your way!

### Self-Check Questions

1. How do you say "This is a book" in Ukrainian? (Answer: **Це книга.**)
2. Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun like **стіл**? (Answer: **він**)
3. Should you use **ти** or **ви** when meeting a teacher for the first time? (Answer: **ви**)
4. Is *«Я є студент»* correct in modern Ukrainian? (Answer: No — say **Я студент** without **є**.)
FIN

module: a1-009
level: A1
sequence: 9
slug: this-is-i-am
version: '2.0'
title: This Is / I Am
subtitle: Zero Copula and Personal Pronouns
duration: 45
transliteration: false
tags: [grammar, pronouns, identity]
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:

grammar:
